content:
Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol Quang Huy Pham a , Yuecheng Zhang a, Bao K. Doan a

, Shinji Kanehashi b, Eirini Goudeli a

, Colin A. Scholes a,* a Department of Chemical Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria, Australiab Department of Applied Chemistry, Tokyo University of Agricultural Technology, Japan

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:PolyimidePolybenzimidazoleFluoropolymerMethanolDimethyl etherWater

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: A B S T R A C T Polymeric membranes have potential for dimethyl ether (DME) separation from the methanol dehydration process as a more energy efficient alternative to distillation. Various polymer classes were studied for their DME, methanol (CH3OH), and water (H2O) permeability and selectivity over a temperature range of 35–200 ◦C. At 35 ◦C, DME significantly plasticized the glassy polymers, with permeability increasing substantially with pres­sure. Polymers with high fractional free volume like Teflon AF 1600 and 6FDA-TMPDA exhibited the highest DME permeabilities around 1000 barrer. However, only the superhydrophobic PVDF-HFP and Teflon AF 1600 demonstrated DME/CH3OH separation factors greater than 1, making them suitable for separating DME. At elevated temperatures above DME’s critical temperature, the plasticization effects diminished as DME transi­tioned from a vapor to gas phase. DME permeability increased with temperature following an Arrhenius rela­tionship, with activation energies inversely related to the polymers’ fractional free volume. The DME/CH3OH separation factor improved at 200 ◦C compared to 150 ◦C for most polymers as DME’s permeability increased. All membranes displayed selectivity for H2O over DME, indicative of H2O smaller kinetic diameter and greater condensation. For H2O/CH3OH separation, all polymers exhibited H2O/CH3OH separation factors between 10 and 1000 at 35 ◦C, with values decreasing at higher temperatures due to reduced H2O solubility.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: mers exhibited H2O/CH3OH separation factors between 10 and 1000 at 35 ◦C, with values decreasing at higher temperatures due to reduced H2O solubility. Teflon AF 1600 demonstrated the most stable H2O/CH3OH separation performance across the temperatures studied. Overall, the results highlighted the potential of conventional gas separation membranes, particularly superhydrophobic perfluorinated polymers, to be utilised for sustainable DME production from methanol dehydration.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: 1. Introduction Dimethyl ether (DME) is regarded as efficient hydrogen (H2) carrier and a potential alternative to domestic fuels due to physical and chemical properties being similar to liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) [1,2]. Furthermore, at 5 bar DME is liquefied at ambient temperature, making DME suitable for use within existing LPG infrastructure [3]. The synthesis of renewable DME from CO2 hydrogenation is also a promising strategy for carbon capture and utilisation [4]. The process consists of methanol (CH3OH) formation from CO2 and then CH3OH dehydration to DME: CO2 hydrogenation : CO2 + 3H2 ⇄CH3OH + H2OΔH0 = -49.5 kJ mol-1(Eq. 1)

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content:
CH3OH dehydration : 2CH3OH ⇄CH3OCH3 + H2OΔH0 = -23.4 kJ mol-1(Eq. 2) The current industrial CH3OH dehydration process is provided in Fig. 1, which involves a fixed-bed reactor followed by two distillation columns in series, delivering high-purity DME of >99.99 wt% [5]. CH3OH dehydration (Eq. (2)) is limited by thermodynamic equilibrium, which restricts the conversion in a single pass, hence the need for DME separation and CH3OH recycle. Separation can be achieved via distil­lation, but it is energy-intensive and involves significant capital and operational costs. Previous studies have demonstrated that the distilla­tion process can account for 40–70 % of the total energy demand of CO2 hydrogenation to CH3OH and DME, highlighting the need for more energy efficient alternatives [6,7]. An attractive strategy is the inte­gration of gas separation membranes in place of distillation, which can offer substantial advantages by selectively separating DME and H2O

Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: Available online 26 July 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). from unreacted CH3OH at pressure. The most appropriate membrane separation mechanism for DME recovery and purification is through solution diffusion within nonporous polymeric membranes [8]. The sorption of penetrant into the polymer matrix is dependent on the chemical affinity and condensability (critical temperature) of the penetrant, while diffusivity within the polymer matrix is influenced by the polymer’s fractional free volume (FFV) and the kinetic diameter of the penetrant [9–11]. These param­eters are provided in Table 1 for the components involved in the CH3OH dehydration to DME process. To the best of the authors knowledge, the permeability of DME through the polymeric films has only been reported for elastomers associated with natural gas infrastructure [12,13]. Elas­tomers may lack the thermal and mechanical stability required for gas separation membranes, as their composition is formulated for their flexibility, not for selectivity. Therefore, there is a need to explore classes of existing polymeric membranes that are more appropriate to the conditions of the CH3OH dehydration process.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: fore, there is a need to explore classes of existing polymeric membranes that are more appropriate to the conditions of the CH3OH dehydration process. This investigation was able to assess the viability of polymeric membranes for DME separation as well as selectivity against CH3OH, as an alternative to the existing industrial CH3OH dehydration process (Fig. 1). Various classes of polymers used as gas separation membranes were evaluated based on their permeability and selectivity for efficient DME and H2O separation. The choice of polymers was based on their thermal resistance, as the exit gas from the fixed bed reactor is at elevated temperature. The underlying interaction of DME within these membranes was characterised and the potential for specific membranes to be utilised at elevated temperature conditions discussed. Importantly, the outcome highlighted that conventional gas separation membranes can be incorporated into DME production and therefore contribute to the production of a sustainable fuel. 2. Experimental 2.1. Material and polymeric membrane preparation DME was purchased as a liquefied gas (purity ≥99.9 %, SigmaAldrich Inc. USA) and liquid CH3OH was supplied by Chem-Supply (Analytical grade). Deionized H2O was generated using a Merck Milli­pore water purification system. Powdered cellulose diacetate (CDA) and cellulose triacetate (CTA) were supplied by Daicel Corporation, Japan.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: ck Milli­pore water purification system. Powdered cellulose diacetate (CDA) and cellulose triacetate (CTA) were supplied by Daicel Corporation, Japan. The fabrication of ~60 μm thick dense cellulose acetate membranes followed the solution casting procedures described in the literature [15]. Polysulfone (PSF) with Mw 37000–45000 was purchased from BASF Australia Limited. Powder PSF was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran to make up 2.5 wt% solution and then cast on glass Petri dish at near saturated condition until dried. The PSF was further annealed in a vacuum oven at 60 ◦C for 48 h to remove residual solvent, and store in desiccator for at least a week before test to minimize physical aging effects. Similarly, powered PVC (high Mw, Merck) was prepared into ~50 μm thick film using the same solvent casting method. PVDF-HFP pellets (Mw ~400,000, Merck) were dissolved in acetone to create a 2.5 wt% solution, which was then cast onto a glass Petri dish. The solution was left in a near-saturated environment until a ~60 μm film formed. This film was then vacuum-dried at 60 ◦C to completely remove any solvent within the film and stored in a vacuum desiccator until testing. Teflon AF 1600 film was prepared by dissolving in Novec 7200 Engineered Fluid (3 M, Australia) to make up a 2.5 wt% solution. This solution was filtered through glass wool and a polytetrafluoroethylene membrane filter.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: red Fluid (3 M, Australia) to make up a 2.5 wt% solution. This solution was filtered through glass wool and a polytetrafluoroethylene membrane filter. It was then cast on cellophane within a glass ring and left to evaporate for at least two days at room temperature. The ~50 μm thick film was subsequently annealed at elevated temperature until 150 ◦C under vacuum for 48 h to ensure complete solvent removal and stored in a vacuum desiccator. Polyimide films, including Matramid5218 (Huntsman) and laboratory-synthesised 6FDA-TMPDA, were prepared using the solvent casting method as described in our previous studies [16]. Yellow powder copolyimide P84 was obtained from Evonik and dissolved in dime­thylformamide to create a 2.5 wt% solution. This solution was casted onto a glass Petri dish and allowed to dry under an inert gas flow environment at room temperature. After forming a ~50 μm thick film, it was transferred to a vacuum oven and annealed at 150 ◦C for 48 h. The dried membrane was then stored in a desiccator for a week before testing. Polybenzimidazole (PBI) membrane was provided by PBI Perfor­mance Products Inc in flat sheet configuration. It was dried in a vacuum oven at 200 ◦C overnight to remove any residual solvent. Polydimethyl siloxane (PDMS) composite membranes, which is known as PERVAP 4060, was purchased from Sulzer Chemtech (Switzerland). These membranes consisted of a PDMS layer, approximately 10 μm thick, supported by a highly microporous substrate.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: from Sulzer Chemtech (Switzerland). These membranes consisted of a PDMS layer, approximately 10 μm thick, supported by a highly microporous substrate. The cross-linked PDMS film was used as received, without any additional purification. The chemical structure of the polymers and their glass transition temperatures (Tg) are presented in Table 2.

Fractional free volume (FFV) of selected polymers were determined using the Bondi group contribution technique [28], with their densities initially measured using the standard buoyancy method [29]. The FTIR spectra of examined polymeric membranes are represented in Fig. 2 and are consistent with literatures, with the specific functional groups of each membrane detailed in Table 3. The cross-sectional images of the examined polymeric membranes were obtained using a Helium Ion Microscopy (HIM) (Zeiss ORION NanoFab, Germany), which are provided in Fig. 3. All membranes exhibit a dense structure with lack of any notable defects.

2.2. Permeation test The DME permeability of the membranes was assessed over a tem­perature range of 35–200 ◦C using the constant volume, variable pres­sure (CVVP) method [39,40]. All polymers were vacuum dried in the membrane module overnight to ensure the removal of residual gases.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content:
Since the vapor pressure of liquefied DME at ambient temperature is less than 6 bar, a pressure range of 3–5 bar was used for the single perme­ation test. The time-lag method was used to determine the DME diffu­sion coefficients from the initial permeability logging data [29,39]. For the vapor permeation measurements of H2O and CH3OH, a modified variable volume constant pressure (VVCP) apparatus was used, as in our prior studies [41,42]. Helium gas (purity ≥99.99 %, BOC Australia) was selected as carrier gas and bubbled through deionized H2O or liquid CH3OH, with the gas stream then passing through a demister to eliminate any entrained droplets. Both the bubbler and demister were submerged in a temperature-controlled bath (Poly­Science) and partially filled with stainless steel beads to enhance mass transfer efficiency. The vapor activity of H2O and CH3OH was controlled by adjusting the bubbler temperature. To reduce concentration polari­zation, the feed gas flow rate was set to 1300 mL(STP)/min, while the sweep gas flow rate was maintained at 76 mL(STP)/min [43]. The vapor composition in the feed was estimated with the Antoine equation, and

Both water and methanol vapor activity at feed side were determined by the ratio of vapor partial pressure and the vapor saturated pressure (p0) at operating temperature T: a = pp0

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: (Eq. 3) where the partial pressure can be estimated using Antoine equation: log(p) = A -BC + T(Eq. 4) where p (mmHg) is the vapor partial pressure at water bath temperature Twb (oC); A, B, and C are the Antoine constants for water and methanol at water bath temperature Twb. The time-lag method was used to estimate the diffusivity of the membranes from the initial permeability data [44]. To avoid thermal degradation of polymeric materials, only CTA, Teflon AF 1600, Matrimid5218, 6FDA-TMPDA and PBI were considered for permeation testing beyond 150 ◦C. Each permeation test was repeated with at least 2 independent membranes, and the standard deviation was used to determine error. 3. Results and discussions 3.1. DME permeability The permeability of DME through the membranes at 35 ◦C is pro­vided in Fig. 4, with PDMS demonstrating the highest DME perme­ability, which is consistent with that reported by Orme et al. [45]. The polyimide 6FDA-TMPDA and CDA had the next highest permeabilities, followed by Teflon AF 1600, even though Teflon AF 1600 had a greater FFV (Table 4), demonstrating that solubility of DME plays a crucial role in permeability at this temperature. Further evidence for the strong role of solubility is the pressure dependent behavior for all the polymers tested. This behavior revealed that DME at 35 ◦C clearly plasticized the glassy polymers studied, with CTA, Matrimid5218 and P84 the most susceptible.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: tested. This behavior revealed that DME at 35 ◦C clearly plasticized the glassy polymers studied, with CTA, Matrimid5218 and P84 the most susceptible. For rubbery PVDF-HFP, the swelling induced by DME raised permeability by almost an order of magnitude. This behavior indicated that DME’s plasticization pressure at 35 ◦C was less than 3 bar for a wide class of polymers. The corresponding DME diffusivity in these membranes at 35 ◦C is provided in Fig. 4(ii), which also demonstrated the pressure dependence behavior. The increased diffusivity at elevated pressure was due to DME induced polymer plasticization/swelling, which increased FFV due to polymer chain mobility [9,40]. If the penetrant concentration within the polymer is assumed proportional to the upstream pressure, then diffu­sivity (D) can be described by [29,46]: D = D0.eγ.p(Eq. 5) where D0 is diffusion coefficient at infinite dilution, p is the upstream pressure and γ is the plasticization potential. A higher γ demonstrates a more pronounced plasticization effect or greater swelling of the polymer [46]. The corresponding D0 and γ values for DME within the membranes are provided in Table 4. Polymers with higher FFV tend to have higher DME diffusion co­efficients but lower plasticization potential. For example, whereas pol­yimide Matrimid5218 with a moderate FFV (0.151) had a low D0 and high γ (2.03), the comparable polyimide 6FDA-TMPDA had a higher D0 and a lower γ (0.20).

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: ide Matrimid5218 with a moderate FFV (0.151) had a low D0 and high γ (2.03), the comparable polyimide 6FDA-TMPDA had a higher D0 and a lower γ (0.20). This was also demonstrated for the two fluorinated polymers, Teflon AF 1600 with the highest FFV (0.302) had the sec­ond highest D0 and a low γ (0.35), while PVDF-HFP with a FFV almost half that of Teflon AF 1600, had a D0 that was four orders of magni­tude lower, and a high γ (1.85). DME is a weak base that can act as a proton receiver due to the presence of the lone pair of electrons on the oxygen [47]. Therefore, DME can form hydrogen bonds with other hydrogen donors such as hydroxyl (-OH) or amine (-NH) groups. This explains the high plastici­zation potential for PSF, compared to PBI, even though their diffusion coefficients are of the same magnitude. Moreover, DME has a polar C–O bond due to the difference in electronegativity between carbon and oxygen. This polarity allows DME to engage in dipole-dipole interactions with common polar functional groups in polymers, such as carbonyl (C––O), ester (COO), or nitrile (C –––N) groups. Hence, polymers with

Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489 these functional groups showed much higher plasticization potential; for example, CTA compared to CDA, which is attributed to the additional acetate group within CTA. The permeability of DME at elevated temperatures (150 and 200 ◦C) is provided in Fig. 5, and there was clear difference in the permeability – pressure relationship at these elevated temperatures.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: res (150 and 200 ◦C) is provided in Fig. 5, and there was clear difference in the permeability – pressure relationship at these elevated temperatures. DME permeability for all membranes was essentially constant across the pressures studied at 200 ◦C, and for 150 ◦C, only Matrimid5218, CTA and PBI demon­strated pressure-dependent permeability. Furthermore, the increase in permeability for these three polymers was not as significant as that observed at 35 ◦C. This is associated with higher temperatures reducing penetrant solubility at a set pressure, and therefore increasing the cor­responding plasticization pressure [40,48], which for 200 ◦C would be beyond 5 bar for all the polymers studied here. Teflon AF 1600 and 6FDA-TMPDA maintained the highest DME permeability, in the range of 102 to 103 barrer and PBI continued to show the lowest permeability, ~10 barrer. CDA could not be studied at these temperatures due to thermal degradation. Interestingly, at 150 ◦C the permeability in 6FDA-TMPDA, Teflon AF 1600 and CTA experi­enced a lower value than at 35 and 200 ◦C, especially for the maximum pressure studied of 5 bar. This was most extreme for 6FDA-TMPDA, where permeability decreased by an order of magnitude, from 1176 barrer to 106 barrer at 3 bar pressure. This behavior was associated with changing solubility of DME, as conditions transition from vapor to gas, through the critical temperature (Table 1).

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: This behavior was associated with changing solubility of DME, as conditions transition from vapor to gas, through the critical temperature (Table 1). Specifically, below the crit­ical temperature, the increase in DME diffusivity with rising tempera­ture was much less than the decrease in solubility within the membrane, due to DME being a vapor. As a result, the permeability decreased. Above the critical temperature, diffusivity increase was significantly greater than the solubility decrease, as DME was a gas, and hence the resulting permeability was higher. PBI does not demonstrate this behavior, indicating that diffusivity dominated the permeance of DME through this membrane, irrespective of temperature, due to the low FFV limiting solubility. The corresponding DME diffusivity as a function of temperature is provided in Fig. 6 for 3 bar and demonstrates an increase in diffusivity with temperature for all membranes. For example, the diffusivity in 6FDA-TMPDA increased by almost a factor of two when temperature increased from 35 to 150 ◦C, rising from 8.69 x 10-7 to 1.52 x 10-6 cm2/ s, while PBI diffusivity increased by 2 orders of magnitude from 35 to

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content:
Fig. 3. Cross-sectional HIM images of CTA, CDA, Matrimid 5218, P84, 6FDA-TMPDA, PBI, PSF, and PVDF-HFP membranes. Q.H. Pham et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489

200 ◦C, rising from 1.58 x 10-11 to 1.48 x10-9 cm2/s. DME diffusivity in the membranes followed an Arrhenius relation­ship with temperature, and the corresponding activation energy are provided in Table 5. Polymers with high FFV require lower activation energy for diffusion. Thus, 6FDA-TMPDA and Teflon AF 1600 exhibited much lower activation energy values compared to the low FFV polymers, such as PBI and CTA. This was attributed to the increased restrictions and interactions the large DME molecule would experience transposing through a low FFV polymeric morphology. 3.2. DME/CH3OH separation The CH3OH permeability of the membranes at 35 ◦C as a function of vapor activity is presented in Fig. 7. PDMS exhibited the highest CH3OH permeability, comparable to other reported vapor permeabilities through this rubbery polymer [27,49]. Matrimid5218, and 6FDA-TMPDA exhibit high CH3OH permeability as well, which supports the literature [16], and is associated with high solubility. PSF and cel­lulose acetate membranes show permeability in the range of 102 barrer,

Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489 while Teflon AF 1600 exhibits CH3OH permeability in the range of 10 barrer, which is considerably different from the trend observed for DME (Fig. 2). This is associated with the superhydrophobic state of Teflon AF 1600 having poor methanol solubility [50]. Both PBI and P84 copolyimide demonstrated a permeability of ~1 barrer. Only PVDF-HFP demonstrates an activity dependent permeability behavior, rising from 0.45 barrer (0.6 activity) to 0.7 barrer (0.9 activity), which is indicative of swelling. In general, the permeability of CH3OH is more than an order of magnitude greater than that of DME for most of the membranes, due to CH3OH smaller kinetic diameter and much higher condensability (crit­ical temperature) [45]. However, PVDF-HFP, Teflon AF 1600, and PDMS demonstrate a DME/CH3OH separation factor greater than 1 (Fig. 8), which is attributed to the superhydrophobic nature of Teflon AF 1600 limiting CH3OH solubility more than DME, while the rubbery hydrophobic nature of PVDF-HFP and PDMS clearly favors sorption of DME. Therefore, membranes based on PVDF-HFP, Teflon AF 1600, and PDMS have the potential for use in DME/CH3OH separation pro­cesses under ambient conditions, where DME is separated into the

content: Q.H. Pham et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489 permeate stream and the unreacted CH3OH can be returned to the reactor at pressure. The permeability of CH3OH at elevated temperatures is provided in Table 6, for low activity given the temperature conditions. There is a trend of decreasing CH3OH permeability with increasing temperature for most studied polymers, which is attributed to reduced CH3OH sol­ubility at the higher temperature. However, CTA and Teflon AF 1600 have increased CH3OH permeability, indicative that diffusivity has a much stronger role in these two polymers, which is expected for Teflon AF 1600 given the superhydrophobic nature of the polymer. At 150 ◦C, the DME/CH3OH separation factors show reductions in selectivity for CH3OH against DME for most polymers such as 6FDATMPDA and P84. Notably, Teflon AF 1600 separation factor is reduced to almost 1, compared to the 35 ◦C result. This reduction in separation factor at elevated temperatures demonstrated a decrease in the solubility selectivity of Teflon AF 1600 for DME over CH3OH. At 200 ◦C, the DME/CH3OH separation factors all demonstrate an improvement in selectivity for DME compared to 150 ◦C. For example, 6FDA-TMPD DME/CH3OH separation factor increased from 0.03 to 0.2 (or alternatively the CH3OH/DME separation factor reduced from 33 to 5). Teflon AF 1600 maintained a separation factor greater than 1, which improved at the higher temperature.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: H/DME separation factor reduced from 33 to 5). Teflon AF 1600 maintained a separation factor greater than 1, which improved at the higher temperature. The shift in separation factor from 150 ◦C to 200 ◦C showed the temperature increase in DME permeability outpaces the increase in CH3OH permeability. This was attributed to the critical temperature of DME (126.8 ◦C) being exceeded at 150 ◦C, meaning DME behaves as a gas at these elevated tempera­tures. In contrast, CH3OH, with a critical temperature of 240.8 ◦C, re­mains as a vapor. As a gas, DME experienced reduced interaction with itself and the polymeric matrices, leading to enhanced diffusivity. On the other hand, CH3OH as a vapor, has solubility associated permeability, which was hindered at the elevated temperature.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: 3.3. DME/H2O separation factor The H2O permeability of the different polymeric membranes at 35 ◦C, as a function of H2O activity are provided in Table 7. These permeabilities are in good agreement with the authors’ prior measure­ments [15,43,50,51]. Polymers such as 6FDA-TMPDA, CTA and CDA demonstrated the highest H2O permeability, ranging between 104 to 105 barrer. This high H2O permeability demonstrate that these polymers allow substantial H2O vapor transport due to their relatively large FFV and favorable interactions, as is the case for the cellulose acetate poly­mers. The rubbery PDMS also has a high H2O permeability due to H2O ability to diffuse through the hydrophobic matrix [49], but a much lower permeability is observed for rubbery PVDF-HFP, which is asso­ciated with the superhydrophobic nature of this fluorinated polymer. The corresponding H2O/DME separation factors are provided in Fig. 9, which demonstrates that all polymers were selective for H2O. This performance is expected given the high condensability of H2O as well as small kinetic diameter [11]. PDMS and Teflon AF 1600 had the lowest H2O/DME separation factors, 2.4 and 3.8 respectively, which is attrib­uted to their hydrophobic nature limiting water sorption [50], while PBI had the greatest H2O/DME separation factor, due to low FFV enabling the smaller H2O to readily permeate compared to the larger DME. The corresponding change in H2O/DME separation factors at elevated temperatures is provided in Fig.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: 2O to readily permeate compared to the larger DME. The corresponding change in H2O/DME separation factors at elevated temperatures is provided in Fig. 9, based on water activity of 0.008. It is difficult to directly compare H2O permeability values for the temperature range studied, due to the significant difference in water activity of the measurements (from 0.6 to 0.008). Q.H. Pham et al. Journal of Membrane Science 734 (2025) 124489 temperature increased, expect for Teflon AF 1600. This reduction in selectivity is attributed to lower H2O solubility within the polymers at elevated temperature, which resulted in significant reduction in H2O permeability; much more than changes in DME permeability at elevated temperature. Teflon AF 1600 selectivity variation is attributed to the unusual behavior of water within this membrane [50]. Importantly, all membranes still retain the ability to separate H2O from DME, with PBI retaining the highest selectivity.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: 3.4. H2O/CH3OH separation factor The corresponding H2O/CH3OH separation factor for these mem­branes is provided in Fig. 10, based on a water activity of 0.6. There is a clear trade-off between permeability and separation factor for H2O/ CH3OH, where high FFV also corresponds to high permeability for CH3OH and hence a low separation factor. All polymers demonstrate a selectivity for H2O over CH3OH, and hence can be used to selectively remove H2O from the DME process and enable recycling of unreacted CH3OH. At elevated temperatures, the H2O/CH3OH separation factors are lower for all of the membranes studied, as shown in Fig. 10. The membranes lose their selectivity towards H2O, as the elevated temper­atures decrease the solubility of H2O within the polymers to a greater extent than CH3OH. Diffusivity selectivity is expected to favor H2O over CH3OH at elevated temperatures, due to the smaller kinetic diameter (Table 1). When the temperature was raised from 150 to 200 ◦C, H2O permeability and H2O/CH3OH separation factor in most of the selected polymers decreased further. This trend is indicative of the diminishing differential sorption and diffusivity of H2O and CH3OH at higher tem­peratures, where both species behave more similarly in the polymer matrix. In our previous study, we demonstrated that higher tempera­tures led to a significant reduction in H2O and CH3OH sorption between 100 and 200 ◦C and thus, a decrease in permeability for polyimide and PBI-based membranes [16].

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: ignificant reduction in H2O and CH3OH sorption between 100 and 200 ◦C and thus, a decrease in permeability for polyimide and PBI-based membranes [16]. The overall decline in separation factor across most polymers in­dicates that higher temperatures present challenges for the efficient separation of H2O from DME and CH3OH, and therefore the water removal process will need to be undertaken at lower temperatures than the CH3OH dehydration reaction, particularly at temperatures below 200 ◦C. Teflon AF 1600 is the only membrane to demonstrate rela­tively stable H2O/CH3OH separation performance across the tempera­tures studied, making it the most suitable material for this application. Importantly, the membrane process will be able to selectively separate H2O into the permeate stream, enabling CH3OH to remain at pressure in the retentate for recycling back to the reactor. 4. Conclusion Membrane gas separation has been demonstrated as a viable DME separation technology in the CH3OH dehydration process. High freevolume and hydrophobic polymers such as Teflon AF 1600 and 6FDA-TMPDA exhibit substantial DME permeability, but only super­hydrophobic perfluorinated PVDF-HFP and Teflon AF 1600 had a DME/CH3OH separation factor above 1. At low temperatures, the polymeric membranes displayed significant DME plasticization at pressures of just 3 bar, indicative of strong solubility dependent behavior from DME.

citation.title: Polymeric membranes for dimethyl ether separation from methanol

content: polymeric membranes displayed significant DME plasticization at pressures of just 3 bar, indicative of strong solubility dependent behavior from DME. This plasticization disappeared when permeability was undertaken above the critical temperature, due to the transition from vapor to gas. Importantly, only Teflon AF 1600 retained a DME/ CH3OH separation factor suitable for the CH3OH dehydration process at the temperatures studied. The permeation of H2O through the membranes demonstrated a strong correlation between FFV and H2O permeability, with the corre­sponding H2O/DME separation factor between 2.4 and 10,100, while H2O/CH3OH separation factors ranged between 10 and 1000. Both the H2O/DME and H2O/CH3OH separation factors decreased at elevated temperatures, due to reduction in the solubility of H2O compared to DME or CH3OH. The most suitable candidate for H2O separation depended on the need for high permeance or high selectivity, with all studied polymeric membranes capable. Specifically, 6FDA-TMPDA notable water permeability, and Teflon AF 1600 excellent water/ methanol selectivity at high temperature make them ideal candidates for methanol dehydration. Importantly, this investigation demonstrated that conventional polymeric membranes can be used in DME separation and contributed to a more sustainable approach a renewable fuel production.
